title_en: The Fourth and Fifth Pillars: Belief in the Messengers and the Last Day
title_ar: الركنان الرابع والخامس: الإيمان بالرسل واليوم الآخر
content_en:
Al-Uthaymin addresses the fourth pillar — belief in the messengers — by first establishing the necessity of prophethood. Since human reason is limited and human beings are inclined to follow desires, Allah in His mercy sent messengers to convey guidance. He notes that the messengers were chosen by Allah, not self-appointed, and were given divine protection from major sins in the fulfilment of their mission (though not from the full range of human experiences, as they were men who ate, slept, fell ill, and died).

The Quran identifies twenty-five prophets by name, and the Sheikh discusses the most prominent among them. He dwells especially on the qualities that Allah required in His messengers: truthfulness (sidq), trustworthiness (amanah), communication of the message (tabligh), and intelligence (fatanah). These qualities are affirmed by the Quran and the authentic hadith and are the basis for trusting the revelation they delivered.

A central point of this section is that Muhammad, peace be upon him, is the Seal of the Prophets (khatam al-nabiyyin), and no prophet or messenger will come after him. This means that the Quran and his authenticated Sunnah are the final and complete guidance for all of humanity until the Day of Judgment. Al-Uthaymin connects this to the obligation of following the Prophet's example in every area of life and warns against any claim of new prophethood after him.

Belief in the messengers also requires not distinguishing between them in terms of belief — a Muslim must affirm all the prophets sent by Allah, as stated in the Quran: 'We do not differentiate between any of His messengers' (2:285). However, the messengers had different ranks with Allah, with Ibrahim and Muhammad holding the highest distinctions as the two ulul-azm (possessors of firm resolve) particularly emphasized in Islamic theology.

The fifth pillar — belief in the Last Day (al-yawm al-akhir) — encompasses everything that will occur after death: the questioning in the grave, the punishment and comfort of the barzakh (the intermediate state), the blowing of the trumpet, the resurrection, the gathering, the reckoning, the scales, the bridge over Hellfire (as-sirat), intercession (shafa'ah), and the final abode in either Paradise or Hellfire. Al-Uthaymin treats each of these in turn.

He stresses that these are matters of the unseen that must be believed as the Quran and Sunnah describe them, without rationalistic reinterpretation. He specifically addresses the reality of the punishment of the grave and its comfort, affirming them against those who deny them. He also discusses the major signs of the Hour — including the Dajjal (Antichrist), the descent of Isa ibn Maryam, the emergence of Gog and Magog, and the rise of the sun from the west — drawing on the authentic hadith literature.

The chapter emphasizes that consistent awareness of the Last Day is a spiritual anchor: a person who remembers the resurrection, the reckoning, and the eternal consequences of his deeds will be motivated to worship sincerely, to avoid sin, and to concern himself with what truly matters.
